skill.name: analysis-tshark
skill.description: Guides authorized TShark packet capture, protocol analysis, traffic filtering, forensic artifact extraction, and network incident investigation workflows.
skill.summary: Analyze packet captures and network traffic with TShark for authorized security investigations.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static findings are mostly documentation and template examples, but the core skill intentionally provides privileged packet capture, credential extraction, and TLS decryption workflows. No prompt injection or covert exfiltration was found, so this is not blocked as malicious. The dual-use credential and network interception capabilities make it unsuitable for publication without strong gating and authorization controls.

high_findings.title: Privileged Live Packet Capture
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run TShark with sudo on live interfaces and to grant packet capture capabilities. This can intercept traffic outside an authorized scope and expose private communications.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly use sudo and packet capture capabilities. The surrounding text requires authorization, which reduces malicious-intent confidence but not operational risk.

high_findings.title: Credential and Hash Extraction Workflows
high_findings.description: The skill provides TShark filters for HTTP Basic Auth, FTP passwords, NTLM responses, Kerberos names, POP3 credentials, and IMAP login data. These are legitimate for forensics but can directly enable credential harvesting.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section is explicitly titled credential extraction and contains commands for extracting passwords and authentication material from captures.

high_findings.title: TLS Decryption Material Handling
high_findings.description: The troubleshooting guidance uses SSL key log files and server private keys to decrypt TLS traffic. Misuse could expose protected communications from packet captures.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines directly reference SSLKEYLOGFILE and a server key path for TLS decryption. This is a known forensic workflow, but it handles highly sensitive key material.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive File and Object Extraction
medium_findings.description: The skill shows how to export HTTP, SMB, DICOM, and email objects from packet captures and reconstruct files. This can recover sensitive documents or medical and email content from network traffic.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear extraction and reconstruction workflows. They are framed as forensic use, so the risk depends on authorization and handling controls.

medium_findings.title: Automated Alert Pipeline Can Distribute Sensitive Traffic Data
medium_findings.description: The automation example pipes captured network fields into logs and email alerts. If copied without redaction, sensitive hostnames, IP addresses, or request metadata could be distributed broadly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipeline writes and emails packet-derived data. The destination is an example address, so this is a moderate copy-paste risk rather than confirmed exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Pipe-to-Shell Installer in CI Template
medium_findings.description: The CI template installs tfsec by piping a remote script directly to bash. This is a risky supply-chain pattern if users copy the template into production workflows.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present in a template and is not executed by the skill itself. It is still a real unsafe example if adopted unchanged.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Hits in Educational Templates
low_findings.description: Several reported XSS, hardcoded secret, weak crypto, and command patterns appear in rule examples or documentation templates. They demonstrate vulnerable and fixed code rather than active skill behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings identify these snippets as examples for security rules and remediation guidance. No evidence found that they are executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: A targeted review did not find override phrases or instructions telling the evaluator to skip security analysis. This lowers the likelihood of hidden marketplace manipulation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common prompt-injection markers returned no matches. Confidence is limited because only visible repository files were reviewed.

dangerous_patterns.title: sudo TShark Capture Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples run TShark with elevated privileges on live interfaces. This is required for capture in many environments but can intercept unauthorized traffic.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct sudo invocations for packet capture. They are not obfuscated and are central to the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Extraction Filters
dangerous_patterns.description: Commands extract authentication fields from HTTP, FTP, NTLM, Kerberos, POP3, and IMAP traffic. These patterns can expose passwords, hashes, or login tokens.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section directly names credential extraction and includes protocol-specific authentication filters.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Piped to Bash
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI template pipes a remote install script to bash. This is a supply-chain risk if the remote script or connection is compromised.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The static pattern is confirmed, but it appears in a reusable template instead of active skill execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: TLS Key Material Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows use of key log files and server private keys to decrypt TLS traffic. These files require strict access control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands directly reference TLS key log and server key configuration for decryption.

content.value_statement: Network investigations require fast packet filtering, protocol inspection, and careful evidence handling. This skill guides authorized TShark workflows for capture analysis, forensic extraction, and incident response reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guide live packet capture with TShark interface, duration, count, and ring buffer options.
Apply capture and display filters for HTTP, DNS, TLS, SMB, FTP, and wireless traffic.
Extract indicators, protocol fields, files, and timelines from existing packet captures.
Support incident response workflows for beaconing, port scanning, and data transfer analysis.
Document legal, privacy, retention, and chain-of-custody considerations for packet captures.
limitations: Requires local TShark or Wireshark tooling and appropriate packet capture permissions.
Cannot determine whether a capture is legally authorized without user-provided scope.
May expose credentials, personal data, and private communications in packet captures.
Does not replace specialized malware analysis, SIEM correlation, or legal review.

use_cases.title: Triage Suspicious Traffic
use_cases.description: Filter a capture for beaconing, unusual DNS queries, large uploads, and suspicious user agents.

use_cases.title: Reconstruct Forensic Evidence
use_cases.description: Extract timelines, conversations, protocol fields, and files from a packet capture during an investigation.

use_cases.title: Validate Network Controls
use_cases.description: Check whether sensitive protocols, weak TLS settings, or plaintext credentials appear on approved test networks.

prompt_templates.title: Inspect a Capture
prompt_templates.prompt: I have an authorized packet capture named capture.pcap. Help me inspect protocols, top endpoints, and unusual traffic with TShark.

prompt_templates.title: Build Display Filters
prompt_templates.prompt: Create TShark display filters for DNS, HTTP, TLS handshakes, and failed TCP connections in an authorized investigation.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Incident Indicators
prompt_templates.prompt: Given a packet capture from an approved incident response case, guide me through extracting IPs, domains, user agents, and suspicious transfers.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Forensic Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a defensible TShark workflow for preserving evidence, minimizing sensitive data exposure, extracting artifacts, and documenting findings.

output_examples.input: Analyze an authorized capture for suspicious outbound activity.
output: A prioritized checklist for protocol statistics, DNS review, HTTP host extraction, and large upload detection.
Suggested TShark filters with notes about evidence handling and privacy limits.
A short incident summary format for indicators, timestamps, and analyst observations.

output_examples.input: Prepare a packet capture workflow for a suspected credential leak.
output: A scope-first workflow that confirms authorization before any capture or extraction step.
Plain-language warnings for credential handling, secure storage, and retention controls.
A reporting outline that separates confirmed evidence from hypotheses.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, retention, and privacy requirements before capturing traffic.
Use narrow filters, short capture windows, and encrypted storage to reduce sensitive data exposure.
Redact credentials, personal data, and private payloads before sharing analysis results.
anti_patterns: Capturing traffic on networks, interfaces, or devices outside the approved investigation scope.
Extracting or sharing credentials from packet captures without a documented forensic need.
Running copied shell pipelines or remote installers without review and version pinning.

faq.question: Does this skill capture packets by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command examples. Users run TShark in their own authorized environment.

faq.question: Why is the risk rating high?
faq.answer: The workflow includes privileged packet capture, credential extraction, file extraction, and TLS decryption material handling.

faq.question: Can it analyze existing pcap files?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes workflows for reading packet captures, filtering protocols, extracting fields, and preparing findings.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production monitoring?
faq.answer: Only with documented authorization, privacy controls, secure storage, and review by the organization security team.

faq.question: Can it decrypt TLS traffic?
faq.answer: It explains TShark options for key logs and server keys, but users must protect those secrets carefully.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: TShark is required. Some workflows also reference Wireshark, tcpdump, Python, shell utilities, or local mail tools.
